summary:
Tokugawa Ieyasu (1543–1616 AD) was the founder and first shogun of the [[event:2336|Tokugawa Shogunate]] — the third of the great unifiers of Japan who transformed a century of civil war (the Sengoku period) into over 250 years of peace, establishing the most durable political system in Japanese history. Ieyasu had survived decades of difficult alliances and near-fatal defeats under [[event:3236|Oda Nobunaga]] and [[event:3237|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], learning patience and political survival as his cardinal virtues — a disposition captured in the proverb attributed to him: 'Life is like a long journey carrying a heavy burden. Do not be in a hurry.' After Hideyoshi's death in 1598, Ieyasu maneuvered among Hideyoshi's designated successors and allies, building a coalition that defeated the western lords at the [[event:2421|Battle of Sekigahara]] (1600) and established him as the dominant power in Japan.

Ieyasu received the title of shogun from the emperor in 1603 — crucially passing it to his son Hidetada just two years later, demonstrating that the title was hereditary in the Tokugawa family and not personally dependent on any single individual. The subsequent [[event:3235|Sieges of Osaka]] (1614–1615) eliminated the Toyotomi family, the last potential rivals. Ieyasu's system of control over the daimyo — requiring them to spend alternating years in Edo (the 'sankin-kōtai' system, requiring their families to reside permanently as hostages in Edo), prohibiting large construction projects without permission, and regulating marriages and alliances — created the surveillance and fragmentation of feudal power that maintained Tokugawa dominance for over two and a half centuries. He died in 1616, shortly after the Osaka campaigns, having seen his system secured.
